Post_Text: You lent the gym for one Saturday. A kid got hurt. The claim landed on your school's policy, not the league's. Here is the paperwork that decides who pays:
A youth league rented the gym for one Saturday. The injured kid's lawyer went straight for the school's policy. (Full newsletter, see linkedin-newsletter.md)
The outside group had its own insurance. The school's carrier still opened the claim file. If that does not make sense, you are missing one document:
A certificate of insurance proves they have a policy. It gives your school zero rights to use it. Most school leaders find that out on the worst possible day:

Lending your gym, field, or auditorium to an outside group? Confirm these four pieces before you hand over a key, or the loss from their activity lands on your school's policy. (Checklist + matrix, see company-page-checklist.md)
Notes: Post from company page; consider PDF lead-capture version

Post_Text: The line typed into the certificate's description box is not an endorsement. And only the endorsement actually protects your school. Here is the difference that costs schools the most:
Walk into most schools and ask who reviews the insurance on the groups using the building. The honest answer is usually nobody. That is not the school's failure. It is the broker's:

Your building rarely sits empty. A church on Sunday. A soccer club on the field. A camp in July. A vendor in the parking lot. Every one of those is a risk transfer that either worked or quietly failed:

Think about the last outside group that used your building. Did you sign an agreement, hold a current certificate, and get named as additional insured by endorsement? Or did someone just hand over a key?
